title: Remove Nodes with a Given Key

content.problemStatement:
You are given a singly linked list containing n nodes.

Each node contains an integer value.

You are also given an integer `key`.

Your task is to **delete all the nodes** from the linked list whose value is **equal to** `key`.

After deleting those nodes, print the final linked list from **head to tail**.

If **all nodes** are deleted and the linked list becomes **empty**, the final output should be −1-1−1.

Function Declaration**Function Name**removeKeyremoveKeyremoveKey — This function removes every node from the linked list whose value matches the given key.

Parametersheadheadhead: A pointer to the **first node** of the linked list.


May be `NULL` if the list is empty.
keykeykey: An integer value.


All nodes whose value equals `key` must be removed.
Return ValueReturns a pointer to the **new head** of the linked list after deleting all matching nodes.


If all nodes are removed (resulting in an empty list), the function returns NULLNULLNULL.
`The input and output formats provided below are only for testing with custom inputs. You only need to return the new head of the linked list. Printing is handled by the driver code.`

Constraints:
1≤T≤1041 \le T \le 10^{4}1≤T≤104
1≤n≤1051 \le n \le 10^{5}1≤n≤105
1≤Node.val≤1051 \le \text{Node.val} \le 10^{5}1≤Node.val≤105
1≤key≤1051 \le \text{key} \le 10^{5}1≤key≤105
The sum of all n across all test cases does not exceed 105\text{The sum of all } n \text{ across all test cases does not exceed } 10^{5}The sum of all n across all test cases does not exceed 105

Input Format
The first line contains an integer `T` — the number of test cases.


For each test case:



The first line contains an integer `n` — the number of nodes in the linked list.
The second line contains `n` integers — the values of the nodes (from head to tail).
The third line contains one integer `key` — the value to be deleted from the list.


Output Format
For each test case:



Print the values of the linked list after deleting all nodes equal to `key`.
If the linked list becomes empty, print `-1`.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1:
Linked list = [10 -> 20 -> 30 -> 20 -> 40 -> 50]
key = 20
Remove all 20’s -> final list = [10 -> 30 -> 40 -> 50]
Test Case 2:
Linked list = [5 -> 5 -> 5 -> 5 -> 5]
key = 5
All nodes are deleted ? print -1
Test Case 3:
Linked list = [1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5 -> 6 -> 7]
key = 8
No node with value 8 -> list remains the same.
Test Case 4:
Linked list = [9 -> 8 -> 9 -> 8]
key = 9
After removing all 9’s -> final list = [8 -> 8]

solution.approach:
The deletion must remove **every occurrence** of key, not just the first one.

The linked list may have:



key at the beginning




key in the middle




key at the end




key not present at all




all nodes equal to key



We need to traverse the entire list once.

STEP-BY-STEP LOGIC**Remove key nodes from the beginning (update head reference)**:

Linked lists require special handling when the key appears at the head.

While head != NULL and head->val == key:

move head = head->next

**If head becomes NULL (all nodes deleted)**:
print -1

continue to next test case

**Remove key nodes from the rest of the list**:
Use a pointer current starting at head

While current->next != NULL:

If current->next->val == key:

delete the next node

link current->next = current->next->next

Else:

move current = current->next

**After processing the entire list**
Print all remaining node values.

WHY THIS METHOD WORKSHandling head separately is necessary because deleting the first node requires updating the head pointer.

Checking current->next ensures we always have access to the node before the one we want to delete.

A single traversal guarantees O(n) time complexity.

Since no extra memory is used, space complexity is O(1).

TIME COMPLEXITYO(n) per test case
(sum of all n ≤ 100000 → safe)

SPACE COMPLEXITYO(1)
